name: behavioral_leadership
description: 7-turn behavioral interview focused on leadership and conflict resolution
candidate_responses: Sure, happy to chat about my leadership experience. I've been in a tech lead role for about 2 years now.

The biggest leadership challenge was when our team of 6 had a major disagreement about whether to rewrite our core service or refactor incrementally. Half the team wanted a clean rewrite, the other half said it was too risky. I organized a structured debate — each side had to present their approach with concrete timelines and risks. Then we did a pilot: spent 2 weeks on each approach with a small module. The data showed the rewrite was faster for that module, but the incremental approach had less risk of breaking production. We ended up doing a hybrid — rewrite the most problematic parts, refactor the rest. Both sides felt heard and the outcome was better than either original proposal.

I think my biggest weakness is that I sometimes take on too much myself instead of delegating. Last quarter my manager actually flagged it — I was working on three critical projects simultaneously and becoming a bottleneck. I've been working on it by being more intentional about pairing junior engineers with tasks I'd normally do myself.

Difficult feedback... yeah. I had to tell a senior engineer that their code quality was slipping. They had been going through personal stuff. I scheduled a private 1-on-1, started by acknowledging their contributions, then shared specific examples — not vague criticism. I said 'I noticed the last three PRs had issues caught in review that weren't typical for you, and I want to check in.' They actually appreciated the directness and shared what was going on. We adjusted their workload temporarily.

I handle disagreements by trying to understand the other person's perspective first. In a recent case, our product manager wanted to ship a feature without proper error handling because of a deadline. Instead of just saying no, I estimated the risk — what would happen if it failed in production. I showed them the on-call burden from the last time we shipped something similar without error handling. They agreed to a two-day delay. I think the key is framing technical concerns in business impact terms.

For cross-functional collaboration, I led a project that involved backend, frontend, design, and data science teams. The challenge was that each team had different priorities and timelines. I created a shared document with clear ownership, weekly syncs that were actually useful, and a shared Slack channel. The biggest learning was that most miscommunication happens because teams have different mental models of what 'done' means. I started writing explicit acceptance criteria for every handoff point.

Looking back at my growth, I'd say the biggest shift was going from 'I need to be the best coder' to 'I need to make the team more effective.' Early on I'd just fix problems myself. Now I ask 'who on the team should learn this?' I've also gotten better at saying no. Not every request needs to be a yes — sometimes the most impactful thing is protecting the team's focus.
